eyebrow.es: Breakout de alfabetización en IA generativa · Grado 6
eyebrow.vi: Breakout hiểu biết AI tạo sinh · Lớp 6
eyebrow.ar: تحدّي الثقافة في الذكاء الاصطناعي التوليدي · الصف 6
eyebrow.ur: جنریٹِو اے آئی خواندگی بریک آؤٹ · جماعت 6
eyebrow.zh: 生成式 AI 素养破解 · 6 年级

chrome.sub: An AI assistant just gave you an answer — fast, clear, and totally sure of itself. But is it right? Crack four locks to learn how to check before you trust.
chrome.briefP: A chatbot can sound confident and still be wrong. It predicts words that fit — it does not truly know if they are true. Read the clues, then open all four locks. Each lock reveals the reasoning behind the answer.
chrome.footerText: Gen AI Literacy Breakouts · Runs entirely in your browser — no logins, no data collected.
chrome.winP: You learned that an AI can sound sure and still be wrong, why that happens, how to build the verify habit — read it, compare it to a trusted source, then fix or decide — and when to trust versus double-check. Smart detective work.

clues.title: Confident isn't correct
clues.body: A chatbot can give an answer in a calm, sure-sounding voice and still have it be wrong. Sounding confident is not the same as being right. The tone tells you nothing about the facts.

clues.title: Guessing the next word
clues.body: An AI assistant does not truly 'know' facts. It predicts words that usually go together based on patterns. That is why it can invent a date, a name, or a source that does not exist — this is called a hallucination.

clues.title: Don't paste it blind
clues.body: Before you use an AI answer, actually read it. Do not copy and paste it into your work without checking. A quick read catches answers that are off-topic, made up, or don't make sense.

clues.title: Check a trusted source
clues.body: Compare the AI's answer to a source you trust: a textbook, a known encyclopedia, a teacher, or an official website. If a reliable source agrees, you can trust it. If none does, doubt it.

clues.title: Some answers need proof
clues.body: Facts, dates, numbers, quotes, and sources are worth double-checking every time. Simple ideas or brainstorms are lower risk. The bigger the decision, the more you should verify first.

clues.title: Taco Tuesday
clues.body: The cafeteria serves tacos every Tuesday, and the line moves fastest right when the bell rings. Tasty to know — but it will not help you open any lock.

locks.title: Confident but Wrong
locks.q: A chatbot gives you a fact in a very confident voice. What does that confidence prove?
options: The answer must be true, because it sounds so sure
Nothing about whether it is true — you still need to check it
The AI looked it up in a trusted source before answering
locks.reason: A confident tone tells you nothing about the facts. An AI can be totally sure-sounding and still be wrong, so you always check the answer against a trusted source.

locks.title: Why It Can Be Wrong
locks.q: Check EVERY reason an AI assistant's answer might be false.

items.t: It predicts likely words instead of truly knowing the facts
It can invent a name, date, or source that doesn't exist
A confident tone does not guarantee the facts are correct
It always searches a trusted encyclopedia before replying
It can never make a mistake once it sounds sure

locks.reason: AI predicts patterns rather than knowing facts, so it can invent details, and its confident tone is no proof. It does not always check a trusted source, and sounding sure does not make it right.

locks.title: Name the Habit
locks.q: Before you use an AI answer, you compare it to a trusted source to make sure it is true. In one word, this habit is to ______ the answer.
locks.reason: The habit is to verify — to check the answer against a trusted source before you use it. Read it, compare it, then fix or decide.

locks.title: The Verify Habit
locks.q: A chatbot just answered your question. Tap the steps to double-check it in the RIGHT order.

pads.e: 📗 Compare it to a trusted source
🧠 Fix it or decide to use it

locks.reason: First READ the answer instead of pasting it blind. Then COMPARE it to a trusted source. Then DECIDE to fix it or use it. Read, Compare, Decide.
